pattern: Subtracting each one's own rank among the ones from its position turns 'gather k ones with adjacent swaps' into 'gather k numbers to their median with unit steps', so sliding a window of k over that adjusted sequence and costing each window against its median with a prefix sum finds the answer in linear time.
hints: Only the positions of the 1's matter; collect them into an array and think about grouping any k of them into a contiguous block.

If you subtract each one's index within that positions array from its actual position, the cost of adjacent swaps needed to gather a group turns into the cost of moving points to their median on a number line -- and that adjusted array is already sorted, so a window's median is just its middle element.
Slide a window of exactly k over the adjusted array; for each window, compute the total distance to the middle element via prefix sums in O(1), and take the minimum over all windows.

edge_inputs.description: statement example three, already k consecutive ones
single element array, k equal to the only one
k equal to the total number of ones, must gather all of them
a run of k already-consecutive ones exists but extra ones sit elsewhere -- the window must be chosen, not just detected
evenly spaced ones with an even window size, exercising the even-length median split
no zeros at all, every window already costs nothing
two ones separated by a wide gap of zeros at the array's extremes

stress.description: random 0/1 array of moderate length with a random valid k
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 2000))
    nums = [rng.randint(0, 1) for _ in range(n)]
    if sum(nums) == 0:
        nums[rng.randrange(n)] = 1
    ones = sum(nums)
    k = rng.randint(1, ones)
    return [nums, k]

stress.description: larger random 0/1 array biased toward more ones, random valid k
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 3000))
    nums = [1 if rng.random() < 0.6 else 0 for _ in range(n)]
    if sum(nums) == 0:
        nums[rng.randrange(n)] = 1
    ones = sum(nums)
    k = rng.randint(1, ones)
    return [nums, k]

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def minMoves(self, nums, k):
        pos = [i for i, v in enumerate(nums) if v == 1]
        g = [p - i for i, p in enumerate(pos)]
        n = len(g)
        prefix = [0] * (n + 1)
        for i in range(n):
            prefix[i + 1] = prefix[i] + g[i]
        best = None
        for l in range(0, n - k + 1):
            r = l + k - 1
            m = l + (k - 1) // 2
            left_count = m - l
            right_count = r - m
            median = g[m]
            left_sum = prefix[m] - prefix[l]
            right_sum = prefix[r + 1] - prefix[m + 1]
            cost = (median * left_count - left_sum) + (right_sum - median * right_count)
            if best is None or cost < best:
                best = cost
        return best

complexity.time: O(n) after collecting one-positions, where n is the number of ones
